topic: Traits - RPITIT (Return Position impl Trait in Traits)

turns.content: I'm getting this error: trait object cannot be returned as a value. I tried using Box<dyn Trait> but the compiler says it's not allowed here. What went wrong?

The issue is that you're trying to return a fat pointer directly from a function signature instead of returning an actual value. When you want to return a trait object, the function must accept an impl Trait parameter or use RPITIT syntax in its return position. The specific constraint here is that Rust requires explicit ownership handling for polymorphic returns.
So I should just change the return type to Box<dyn Trait>? That's what Java does, right? Just use generics and let the JVM handle it.

That won't work because Rust doesn't allow returning trait objects directly without proper constraints. You need to use RPITIT syntax in your function signature instead of Box<dyn Trait>. The key difference is that RPITIT forces the compiler to check ownership at compile time rather than runtime.
Okay, so I should write something like fn get_data() -> impl Trait? But why can't I just use Box<dyn Trait>?

Box<dyn Trait> fails because you're trying to return a fat pointer without proper ownership guarantees. RPITIT solves this by requiring the function body to actually construct and own the concrete type before returning it. This ensures safety without needing dynamic dispatch overhead.
Got it, so instead of fn get_data() -> Box<dyn Trait>, I should write fn get_data() -> impl Trait? And then in the function body I can just return my concrete struct?

Exactly! You write fn get_data() -> impl Trait and inside the function you return your concrete type. The compiler will automatically coerce it to the trait object when needed. This is much cleaner than using Box<dyn Trait> because you avoid the heap allocation entirely.
Wait, but what if I need to use this in a trait method? Can RPITIT work there too?

Yes! This is exactly what RPITIT was designed for. You can define a trait with methods that return impl Trait instead of associated types or Box<dyn Trait>. The compiler handles the coercion automatically when you call these methods.
That's really cool, but I'm still confused about why Java doesn't do this. It seems like they always have to use generics with explicit type parameters.

Java uses generics and interfaces which require explicit type parameters at the call site. Rust's RPITIT lets you express polymorphism more naturally by letting the compiler infer the concrete types internally. It's like having implicit generics that get resolved during compilation rather than requiring manual specification.
So if I define a trait with a method returning impl Trait, any type that implements the underlying trait can be used without specifying the concrete type?

Correct! When you use RPITIT in your trait definition, you don't need to list all possible implementations. The compiler will accept any concrete type that satisfies the trait bounds and automatically coerce it when needed. This is much more flexible than Java's approach where you'd have to specify every possible generic parameter.

topic: Traits - RPITIT (Return Position impl Trait in Traits)

turns.content: I’m following along with a Rust conference talk and they mention RPITIT — Return Position impl Trait in Traits. Why does this even work this way? I thought it was just about returning an impl Trait from methods?

Well, imagine you have a trait `Reader` with a method that returns something that implements `Read`. At first glance, you might think it’s just the same as using associated types. But actually, RPITIT is more like saying ‘I don’t care what specific type I’m returning, just that it implements this trait.’ It’s kind of like if you had a vending machine that could give out any drink — whether cola or sprite or water.
But wait, isn’t `impl Trait` always the same? I thought it was just a shorthand for a trait object.

Right, but not exactly. When you use `impl Trait`, Rust treats that as a trait object under the hood — essentially a boxed `dyn Trait`. This has some overhead and limits what you can do with the returned value at compile time. RPITIT is more like saying ‘I promise to return something of any type that implements this, but I’ll tell you exactly which one at runtime.’ It’s like if your vending machine tells you it’s giving out a Coke right now.
So, why would anyone want RPITIT over just plain `impl Trait`?

Well, imagine you have a library where the user should be able to return any type that implements your trait. If you use associated types, you limit yourself — maybe only to specific implementations or even enums with variants of different types. But if you use RPITIT, you’re saying ‘I’ll give you any concrete type here as long as it fits the trait,’ which is much more flexible. It’s like a super-flexible vending machine that can change what it gives out without changing its API.
That makes sense, but how stable is this RPITIT feature?

RPITIT was stabilized in Rust 1.46 as a way to make trait objects more ergonomic and less surprising — especially when you want to return concrete types without boxing overhead. It’s definitely considered stable now, so you can rely on it for production code. The core idea is that it gives you the flexibility of `impl Trait` plus compile-time guarantees, which was tricky before.
Okay, thanks! So when should I use associated types and when RPITIT?

Good question. Use associated types if you want to constrain what types can be used — like a `Box<dyn Reader>` that always gives out one specific type. But if you want to hide the exact type and just expose it as something fitting a trait, use RPITIT. It’s all about balancing flexibility with compile-time guarantees.
